card.indications: Prevention or treatment of calcium deficiency, supplementation in osteoporosis, and relief of acid indigestion or heartburn when used as an antacid.
card.mechanism: Provides elemental calcium for bone, muscle, nerve, and cardiac function. In the stomach, calcium carbonate neutralizes hydrochloric acid.
card.dosing: Dose depends on indication and elemental-calcium content. Supplements are commonly divided with meals; antacid doses are taken as needed within product limits. Renal impairment and hypercalcemia risk may require lower dosing or avoidance.
card.routeAdministration: Give orally with food for best absorption. Chew chewable products thoroughly. Separate from interacting medicines by the recommended interval. Confirm elemental-calcium content rather than comparing tablet milligrams alone.
card.contraindications: Hypercalcemia, severe hypercalciuria, or hypersensitivity. Use caution with kidney stones, renal impairment, sarcoidosis, and concurrent vitamin D or calcium products.
card.warnings: Excessive use may cause hypercalcemia, constipation, nephrolithiasis, milk-alkali syndrome, and renal injury. Antacid overuse can mask serious gastrointestinal disease.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Constipation, gas, bloating, nausea, and belching.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Hypercalcemia, arrhythmias, kidney stones, confusion, severe weakness, and renal impairment.
card.interactions: Reduces absorption of levothyroxine, tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, bisphosphonates, and iron. Thiazides may increase calcium levels. Digoxin toxicity risk rises with hypercalcemia.
card.monitoring: Monitor calcium, renal function, bowel pattern, kidney-stone symptoms, and total calcium intake. In long-term therapy, assess vitamin D status and bone-health goals.
card.patientTeaching: Do not exceed the labeled dose. Separate from interacting medicines. Report severe constipation, confusion, weakness, vomiting, irregular heartbeat, or flank pain.
card.nutrition: Take with food and adequate water. Include balanced dietary calcium rather than relying only on supplements. Avoid excessive calcium and vitamin D intake.

card.indications: Short-term treatment and maintenance of duodenal or gastric ulcer, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and conditions with excessive gastric-acid secretion.
card.mechanism: Blocks histamine H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells, reducing basal and stimulated acid secretion and pepsin output.
card.dosing: Dosing varies by indication and route. Oral regimens may be divided or given at bedtime. Reduce dose in renal impairment and in some older adults.
card.routeAdministration: May be given with or without food. Bedtime dosing is common for ulcer therapy. IV doses should be diluted and administered at the labeled rate. Separate from antacids when directed.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cimetidine. Use caution in renal or hepatic impairment and in older adults at risk for confusion.
card.warnings: Can cause mental-status changes, especially in older or critically ill patients. Long-term use may contribute to vitamin B12 deficiency. It inhibits multiple hepatic enzymes and has many interactions.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, dizziness, diarrhea, constipation, fatigue, and rash.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Confusion, hallucinations, bradycardia, hypotension with rapid IV use, blood dyscrasias, liver injury, and severe hypersensitivity.
card.interactions: May increase levels of warfarin, phenytoin, theophylline, certain benzodiazepines, lidocaine, and other CYP-metabolized drugs. Can increase citalopram exposure. Antacids may alter absorption.
card.monitoring: Assess symptom relief, GI bleeding, renal and hepatic function, mental status, CBC if prolonged therapy, and interacting-drug levels or effects.
card.patientTeaching: Report black stools, vomiting blood, confusion, jaundice, or unusual bruising. Check with a pharmacist before adding new medicines because interactions are common.
card.nutrition: Avoid foods and beverages that worsen reflux. Limit alcohol and late meals. Maintain adequate vitamin B12 intake during prolonged therapy.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible bacterial infections, including selected urinary, gastrointestinal, respiratory, skin, bone, and intra-abdominal infections. Use only when indicated because serious adverse effects and resistance are concerns.
card.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and causing bacterial death.
card.dosing: Dose depends on infection, site, organism, route, and renal function. Oral and IV therapy may be switched when clinically appropriate. Renal impairment requires adjustment.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses with water and at evenly spaced times. Do not give with dairy products alone or calcium-fortified juice alone. Separate from antacids, iron, zinc, calcium, magnesium, and sucralfate. Do not crush extended-release tablets.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to fluoroquinolones and concomitant tizanidine use. Use caution with myasthenia gravis, aneurysm risk, seizure disorders, QT prolongation, renal impairment, and tendon disorders.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include disabling tendon, nerve, CNS, and musculoskeletal reactions and worsening myasthenia gravis. Risks also include aortic aneurysm or dissection, dysglycemia, QT prolongation, C. difficile diarrhea, and hepatotoxicity.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, headache, dizziness, abdominal discomfort, and rash.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Tendon rupture, peripheral neuropathy, seizures, severe psychiatric or CNS effects, aortic rupture, severe hypoglycemia, QT-related arrhythmia, liver injury, and severe hypersensitivity.
card.interactions: Chelates with multivalent cations. Tizanidine is contraindicated. Warfarin effect may increase. QT-prolonging drugs, corticosteroids, and hypoglycemic agents increase specific risks. Caffeine and theophylline effects may increase.
card.monitoring: Monitor cultures, clinical response, renal function, glucose in at-risk patients, tendon pain, neuropathy, CNS changes, bowel pattern, liver symptoms, and ECG when risk factors exist.
card.patientTeaching: Stop and report tendon pain, numbness, weakness, severe anxiety or confusion, palpitations, severe diarrhea, or allergic symptoms. Avoid unnecessary sun exposure and complete the full course.
card.nutrition: Maintain hydration. Separate mineral supplements and fortified products from doses. Avoid taking with dairy alone, although dairy may be consumed as part of a regular meal.

card.indications: Treatment of major depressive disorder. It may also be used off label for selected anxiety disorders under prescriber supervision.
card.mechanism: Selectively inhibits serotonin reuptake in the central nervous system, increasing serotonergic activity.
card.dosing: Common adult starting dose is 20 mg once daily; some patients start lower. Maximum dose is generally 40 mg daily, but 20 mg daily is the usual maximum in older adults, hepatic impairment, CYP2C19 poor metabolizers, or with cimetidine or other CYP2C19 inhibitors.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily, with or without food, at a consistent time. Do not stop abruptly; taper gradually when possible.
card.contraindications: Use with MAO inhibitors within required washout periods, linezolid or IV methylene blue when serotonergic interaction cannot be managed, pimozide, or hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for increased suicidal thoughts and behaviors in children, adolescents, and young adults. Risks include QT prolongation, serotonin syndrome, hyponatremia, bleeding, mania, seizures, and discontinuation symptoms.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, dry mouth, sweating, insomnia or somnolence, fatigue, tremor, diarrhea, and sexual dysfunction.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Suicidality, serotonin syndrome, torsades de pointes, severe hyponatremia, abnormal bleeding, mania, seizures, and severe withdrawal symptoms.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors and other serotonergic drugs increase serotonin-syndrome risk. NSAIDs, aspirin, and anticoagulants increase bleeding. Cimetidine and CYP2C19 inhibitors increase exposure. QT-prolonging drugs increase arrhythmia risk.
card.monitoring: Assess mood, suicidality, anxiety, sleep, sodium in at-risk patients, bleeding, ECG and electrolytes when QT risk is present, and signs of mania or serotonin syndrome.
card.patientTeaching: Benefits may take several weeks. Report worsening depression, suicidal thoughts, agitation, fever, tremor, diarrhea, fainting, or palpitations. Do not stop suddenly.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Limit alcohol. Maintain adequate hydration and sodium intake unless otherwise restricted.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible serious anaerobic and gram-positive bacterial infections. Topical and vaginal products are used for acne or bacterial vaginosis depending on formulation.
card.mechanism: Binds to the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis. Usually bacteriostatic but may be bactericidal at higher concentrations against susceptible organisms.
card.dosing: Dose depends on infection, route, and severity. Oral doses are divided; IV dosing is weight and severity based. No routine renal adjustment is usually required, but hepatic disease may require monitoring.
card.routeAdministration: Give capsules with a full glass of water and keep the patient upright to reduce esophageal irritation. Dilute and infuse IV doses at the recommended concentration and rate. Complete the prescribed course.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to clindamycin or lincomycin. Use cautiously with prior antibiotic-associated colitis or significant gastrointestinal disease.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for severe and potentially fatal C. difficile-associated diarrhea and colitis. Severe skin reactions, anaphylaxis, liver injury, and blood dyscrasias may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, unpleasant taste, rash, and injection-site irritation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: C. difficile colitis, anaphylaxis, severe cutaneous reactions, hepatotoxicity, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, and esophagitis.
card.interactions: Neuromuscular blockers may have enhanced effects. Erythromycin may antagonize clindamycin activity. Live bacterial vaccines may be less effective.
card.monitoring: Assess infection response, cultures, bowel pattern during and after treatment, hydration, liver function, CBC with prolonged therapy, and signs of rash or superinfection.
card.patientTeaching: Report watery or bloody diarrhea, severe abdominal cramps, rash, facial swelling, or jaundice. Diarrhea can occur weeks after therapy. Do not self-treat severe diarrhea.
card.nutrition: May take oral doses with food if stomach upset occurs. Maintain fluids. Probiotics should only be used if approved for the patient.
